text: The Enchiridion's second chapter extends the dichotomy to desire itself: Epictetus instructs us to withdraw desire entirely from things not in our power. If you desire what you cannot control — health, wealth, the affection of a particular person — you have made your happiness a hostage. The Stoic move is not to stop caring but to redirect care. You may pursue health through good habits; you may not demand that the body comply. You may pursue friendship through virtue; you may not demand reciprocation. The distinction matters because one path leaves your flourishing intact against all eventualities, while the other makes it permanently fragile.
question: According to the passage, what is the Stoic approach to things outside our control, such as health or friendship?

options.text: To stop caring about them entirely and practice emotional detachment
To pursue them through appropriate means while not demanding a specific outcome
To accept whatever happens and take no action toward improvement
To desire them moderately rather than intensely
